coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional fluorinated carbon dots artificial interface layer coupled with in-situ generated Zn2+ conductor interlayer enable ultra-stable Zn anode
coredata.dc:creator: Ge, Zhaofei
Xu, Laiqiang
Xu, Yunlong
Wu, Jiae
Geng, Zhenglei
Xiao, Xiangting
Deng, Wentao
Zou, Guoqiang
Hou, Hongshuai
Ji, Xiaobo
coredata.dc:description: Stabilizing zinc anode is a systematic project for aqueous zinc ion batteries (ZIBs), which needs to solve many problems such as dendrite growth, corrosion, hydrogen evolution, and other side reactions. It is urgent to develop a protective layer for zinc anode to solve these problems at one time. Based on the results of calculation, a hydrophobic multifunctional fluorinated carbon dots (F-CDs) protective layer with three kinds of zincophilic groups (-CO, -CHO and -F) was constructed on the Zn anode surface. As expected, these zincophilic groups on the F-CDs layer functioned as zincophilic sites to achieve uniform Zn deposition. Especially, the -F group could in-situ promote the formation of ZnF2 under the F-CDs layer and the ZnF2 layer is usually considered as a solid Zn2+ conductor layer to further even Zn deposition. Additionally, the experimental results also demonstrated that the F-CDs layer coupled with in-situ generated ZnF2 interlayer can not only tackle above issues in an integrated way, but also induce the deposition of Zn on the preferred (002) plane. Therefore, the Zn@F-CDs anode exhibits an ultra-long cycling life over 3500 h at 1 mA cm–2, together with an excellent average coulombic efficiency (99.32% for over 1100 h) in half cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorinated carbon dots
Artificial layer
ZnF2
Interface engineering
Aqueous Zn-ion batteries

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: DFT calculations of Zn adsorbed on (a) bare Zn substrate and different adsorption sites of F-CDs: (b) aldehyde group (-CHO), (c) fluorine group (-F) and (d) carbonyl group (-CO). (e) Schematic illustration of Zn deposition on the bare Zn anode and Zn@F-CDs anode.

(a) TEM image and (b) FTIR spectra of F-CDs. (c) XPS survey spectra and the corresponding high resolution (d) C1s, (e) O1s and (f) F1s spectra of F-CDs, respectively. (g) SEM image and the related mapping of the cross-section of the F-CDs layer. (h) Contact angles of the bare Zn anode and the Zn@F-CDs anode.

(a) The long-time cycling performance of bare Zn anode and Zn@F-CDs anode in symmetric cells at 1 mA cm–2.(b) Nucleation overpotential on bare Zn anode and Zn@F-CDs anode at 1 mA cm–2. (c) LSV curves in 1 M Na2SO4. (d) Rate performance of Zn@F-CDs anode at current densities from 0.1 to 6 mA cm–2 with capacities ranging from 0.1 to 6 mAh cm–2. (e) Tafel curves of two electrodes. (f) XRD patterns of the bare Zn anode and Zn@F-CDs anode in symmetric cells at 1 mA cm–2 after 15 cycles. (g) CE tests in half cells. (h) CV curves of half cells at a scan rate of 1 mV s–1. (i) Chronoamperograms at a fixed overpotential of − 150 mV.

(a) High-resolution XPS spectra of Zn@F-CDs anode surface after 50 cycles at 2 mA cm–2, 1 mAh cm–2 with sputtering time for 0 and 50 s (b) HRTEM image and (c) selected-area electron diffraction pattern of the detached cycled Zn@F-CDs anode. (d) HAADF scanning TEM image and the related element mapping.
In-situ optical microscope images of Zn deposition on (a) bare Zn anode and (b) Zn@F-CDs anode at 6 mA cm–2. EPMA mapping images of (c, d) bare Zn anode and (e, f) Zn@F-CDs anode after 15 cycles.

SEM images of the top view of bare Zn anode after (a, b) 15 cycles and (c,d) 50 cycles. SEM images of the top view of Zn@F-CDs anode after (e,f) 15 cycles and (g,h) 50 cycles. Optical microscope images and consistent with height distribution of (i1, i2 and i3) the cycled bare Zn anode and (j1, j2 and j3) the cycled Zn@F-CDs anode after 15 cycles at 1 mA cm–2 with a capacity of 1 mAh cm–2.

(a) CV profiles of full cells. (b) XRD patterns of the anodes in full cells after 60 cycles at 2 A g–1. (c) Self-discharge behavior of the full cell with Zn@F-CDs anode. (d) Rate performance of bare Zn anode and Zn@F-CDs anode in full cells at various current densities. (e) SEM images of (e1) bare Zn anode and (e2) Zn@F-CDs anode after rate test. (f) Long-time cycling performance of full cells at 1 A g–1.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional fluorinated carbon dots artificial interface layer coupled with in-situ generated Zn2+ conductor interlayer enable ultra-stable Zn anode

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Hunan Province for Chemical Power Source, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha 410083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Hunan Province for Chemical Power Source, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Hunan Province for Chemical Power Source, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha, 410083, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Central South University of Forestry and Technology, Changsha 410004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Central South University of Forestry and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Central South University of Forestry and Technology, Changsha, 410004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Stabilizing zinc anode is a systematic project for aqueous zinc ion batteries (ZIBs), which needs to solve many problems such as dendrite growth, corrosion, hydrogen evolution, and other side reactions. It is urgent to develop a protective layer for zinc anode to solve these problems at one time. Based on the results of calculation, a hydrophobic multifunctional fluorinated carbon dots (F-CDs) protective layer with three kinds of zincophilic groups (-CO, -CHO and -F) was constructed on the Zn anode surface. As expected, these zincophilic groups on the F-CDs layer functioned as zincophilic sites to achieve uniform Zn deposition. Especially, the -F group could in-situ promote the formation of ZnF2 under the F-CDs layer and the ZnF2 layer is usually considered as a solid Zn2+ conductor layer to further even Zn deposition. Additionally, the experimental results also demonstrated that the F-CDs layer coupled with in-situ generated ZnF2 interlayer can not only tackle above issues in an integrated way, but also induce the deposition of Zn on the preferred (002) plane. Therefore, the Zn@F-CDs anode exhibits an ultra-long cycling life over 3500 h at 1 mA cm–2, together with an excellent average coulombic efficiency (99.32% for over 1100 h) in half cells.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A hydrophobic and zincophilic fluorinated carbon dots (F-CDs) protective layer is introduced in this work. ZnF2 as a typical Zn2+ conductor can be in-situ formed during the cycling. The bilayer can tackle interface issues and achieve the preferred (002) plane deposition, leading to a robust Zn anode.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Aqueous Zn-ion batteries (AZIBs) are regarded as one of the most competitive candidates to replace lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) in the large-scale energy storage field due to relatively high theoretical capacity, environment friendliness, low cost, intrinsic safety and resource abundance [1]. Nevertheless, amounts of issues need to be addressed urgently to further accelerate the commercialization of AZIBs, such as the undesirable Zn dendrites, severe hydrogen evolution (HER) as well as other uncontrollable parasitic reactions [2]. It is well established that these hazardous issues as mentioned above are related to the electrode/electrolyte interphase, inducing inferior coulombic efficiency (CE), unsatisfactory cycling stability and even a short circuit during long-time cycling [3]. So it is urgent to solve these issues at the same time.

Noting that the interface between anode and electrolyte is also an important research direction, numerous researchers have dedicated to exploring surface/interface engineering, including electrolyte engineering and surface engineering [4–7]. In terms of the former, developing novel electrolytes [8] and electrolyte additives [9] is an excellent research hotspot. For example, Wang et al. achieved reversible aqueous zinc batteries by introducing an alkylammonium salt additive to zinc trifluoromethanesulfonate (Zn(OTF)2) electrolyte [10], which can be conducive to forming the ZnF2-rich SEI layer. As for the latter, many strategies have been focused on searching for materials containing varieties of functional groups (such as -CO, -CHO and -F etc.) to construct artificial protective layers on the Zn anode surface. Chen et al. constructed a fluorine-doped amorphous carbon layer to inhibit Zn dendrites and guide Zn deposition [11]. Especially, -F groups served as zincophilic site can enhance transport kinetics of Zn2+ ions. Zhou et al. designed a multifunctional interface layer with rich -CO groups to adjust Zn deposition behavior by utilizing the nucleophilic properties of carbonyl group in coordination with Zn metal [10]. Our group discovered that the aldehyde group (-CHO) in carbon dots coating has a more negative adsorption energy with Zn2+ compared with the adsorption energy between Zn2+ on the bare Zn anode, owing to the more nucleation sites provided [13]. No wonder that materials with functional groups may play an essential role in addressing the interface issues that the Zn anode meets. Therefore, based on the previous researcher’s reports, we devote to finding a kind of materials equipped with above all three functional groups simultaneously.

Carbon dots as a new type of carbon material are known to us for their abundant surface functional groups, wide source of raw materials, adjustable structure and good dispersion, playing vital roles in the field of energy storage [14–16]. Among all kinds of carbon dots, in particular, it is the fluoro-based carbon dots (F-CDs) that not only have -F groups, but also simultaneously have -CHO and -CO groups attracting our attention. According to the previous researches’ results, surface functional groups on the CDs could endow their ability of excellent Zn2+ ions diffusion [13]. Additionally, F-CDs materials are insoluble and stable in water, which makes it possible to form a dense surface coating layer. There is no doubt that the F-CDs can act as significant candidate coating materials for solving interface issues.

Hence, on the basis of the intersection aldol condensation reaction of p-fluorobenzaldehyde, acetaldehyde and sodium hydroxide, we prepared F-CDs for use. Subsequently, we developed a dense F-CDs layer on the pristine Zn anode in this work. Firstly, we revealed the adsorption energy between Zn2+ and different adsorption sites on F-CDs by density functional theory (DFT) calculations, which demonstrated that the F-CDs has more negative adsorption energy toward Zn2+, showing superb zincophilicity. More interestingly, we also discovered that the ZnF2 layer generated in-situ between the F-CDs protective layer and pristine Zn anode during the cycling process. In addition, it is well established that the ZnF2 layer is beneficial to impede side reactions and can function as solid Zn2+ conductor layer to further guide even Zn deposition [11,17]. Secondly, the surface negative charge of F-CDs materials was confirmed by the Zetal potential test, which is conducive to repelling SO4 2– anions via electrostatic repulsion interaction. The experimental results also displayed that such hydrophobic and zincophilic F-CDs layer could not merely achieve the preferred Zn(002) plane deposition, but also inhibit hydrogen evolution and Zn corrosion. As a result, the Zn@F-CDs anode maintained an excellent cycle life over 3500 h at 1 mA cm–2 in symmetric cells. Notably, the modified Zn||Cu cells also delivered an enhanced average CE (99.32%). Furthermore, an improved electrochemical performance could be obtained in the assembled Zn@F-CDs||NH4V4O10 full cells compared with the bare Zn-based full cells. There is no doubt that constructing the hydrophobic and zincophilic F-CDs materials would be an efficient way to achieve a stable Zn anode.

It is known that the strong zincophiliclicity of materials can effectively capture Zn2+ ions to prevent aggregation on the Zn surface. Therefore, we first take advantage of a simplified graphite model to calculate the interaction between the Zn2+ ion and different adsorption sites of F-CDs as well as the corresponding charge density difference [18]. As contrast, the adsorption energy between the Zn2+ ion and pristine Zn substrate was also calculated. The Zn adsorption energy for pristine Zn substrate is only − 0.43 eV, while for the different adsorption sites of the F-CDs (-CHO, -F, -CO) are − 3.20 eV, − 2.24 eV and − 2.80 eV, respectively ( Fig. 1a-d). The more negative adsorption energy shows better Zn adsorption ability, further revealing that the F-CDs protective layer has excellent zincophilicity, therefore the Zn2+ ions are in favor of adsorbing at the groups of F-CDs instead of bare Zn, which is conducive to reducing the nucleation barrier and achieving even Zn deposition [19]. Especially, based on the zeta test of F-CDs in ethanol and the previous reports, it is clear to know that the surface charge of F-CDs is negative [20], which benefits from its surface functional groups (-CHO, -CO and -F), contributing to repel SO4 2- anions via the electrostatic repulsion interaction to inhibit forming Zn4SO4(OH)6·5H2O (ZHS) [21]. Moreover, the F-CDs materials are basically insoluble in water, while they are highly dispersed in ethanol (Fig. S1), further indicating the potential of application in engineering protective layer to construct robust Zn anode. What’s more, benefiting from its stability in an aqueous environment, the F-CDs layer can prevent Zn anode from contacting directly with aqueous electrolyte. To enhance the anode stability, the bare Zn anode modified with the F-CDs protective layer was fabricated. As illustrated in Fig. 1e, the bare Zn anode is influenced by Zn corrosion, HER and accumulation of insulating by-products, which may be related to the rough Zn anode surface (Fig. S2), unstable thermodynamics and the composition of electrolyte.[3] Moreover, the bare Zn anode usually goes through a 2D diffusion which leads to the rampant Zn dendrites growth during the long time cycling process. By contrast, the existence of the -F group on the F-CDs protective layer can promote the in-situ formation of ZnF2 layer on the Zn anode surface. And the newly formed ZnF2 can be deemed as solid Zn2+ conductor layer to further even Zn deposition [22]. Thus, benefiting the effect of the bilayer, not only a series of side reactions are suppressed, but dendrite-free Zn anode and uniform Zn deposition are obtained.

The F-CDs materials were fabricated through intersection aldol condensation reaction (Detailed information can be seen in the Supporting Information). It can be seen from the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images that the average size of obtained F-CDs is about 2.84 nm ( Fig. 2a) The digital photo of F-CDs is also shown in Fig. S3. Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FTIR) was performed to analyze the surface functional groups of F-CDs (Fig. 2b). The peaks at around 1605 and 1697 cm–1 are related to -CO and -CHO groups stretching [12]. The vibration peaks at nearby 1158 and 1224 cm–1, which can be attributed to C-F stretching. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) explored the surface element composition of F-CDs. Fig. 2c shows that the survey spectra of F-CDs materials manifest that the obtained F-CDs contain C, O and F three elements, of which content is 81.41%, 10.36% and 8.23%, respectively. Additionally, the high-resolution C1s, O1s and F1s spectra of F-CDs are exhibited in Fig. 2d-f, further proving the existence of surface functional groups. Especially, it is worth to note that the C1s spectra show semi-ionic C-F (287.65 eV) [23]. What’s more, a strong peak corresponding to the semi-ionic C-F (687.10 eV) can be also observed in the F1s spectrum [11]. There is no doubt that the semi-ionic C-F bond is a main state in both C1s and F1s spectra, which may play an essential role in the formation of ZnF2.

Subsequently, we coated the F-CDs protective layer on the Zn anode surface (Experimental details are shown in the supporting information). As exhibited in the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image and corresponding three element mapping images (F, O and C element) of the Zn@F-CDs anode, it is clear to see that the thickness of coated F-CDs layer is about 25 µm and the F, O, C elements evenly distribute in the layer (Fig. 2g). In addition, contact angle test was conducted. As displayed in Fig. 2h, the contact angle of the Zn@F-CDs anode and electrolyte solution is 100.93°, while the value of bare Zn anode is only 94.2°, showing a stronger hydrophobicity. Notably, benefiting from the hydrophobic characteristic of Zn@F-CDs surface, Zn corrosion and HER side reaction can be greatly suppressed [19].

symmetric cells are assembled to explore the influence of F-CDs layer on the cycling life of batteries. The assembled Zn@F-CDs symmetric cells exhibit a steady voltage fluctuation over 3500 h under 1 mA cm–2 with a capacity of 1 mAh cm–2 ( Fig. 3a), which are better than many previously reported works (Table S1). Nevertheless, the assembled bare Zn anode symmetric cells are influenced by obvious voltage fluctuation and suffer from a sudden increase of voltage at around 70 h, which may be caused by the unstable interface between electrode and electrolyte troubled by accumulated non-conductive byproducts and the rampant growth of Zn dendrites [24]. Moreover, as displayed in the inset of Fig. 3a, the voltage hysteresis of bare Zn anode is higher than that of Zn@F-CDs. What’s more, we tested the cells with Zn@CMC anode (Fig. S4), its inferior performance further confirms that the F-CDs materials are the most important in the protective layer.

The curves of the nucleation overpotential in symmetric cells were obtained. Fig. 3b shows that the Zn@F-CDs anode only delivers a low nucleation overpotential of 39 mV, which is lower than that of the bare Zn anode (49 mV). It is well established that the nucleation overpotential is related to the energy barrier for Zn nucleation [25]. Therefore, there is no doubt that the F-CDs can reduce the energy barrier, which may benefit from the zincophilic functional groups on the Zn@F-CDs anode surface. To evaluate the suppression of Zn@F-CDs anode on hydrogen evolution reactions (HER) under 1 M Na2SO4 solution, the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) tests were conducted (Fig. 3c). The Zn@F-CDs anode presents a more negative potential (−1.758 V vs. −1.729 V) response at the same current density, suggesting that the F-CDs protective layer has a better ability to inhibit HER [26]. Fig. 3d shows the rate performance of the assembled bare Zn symmetric cells and the assembled Zn@F-CDs symmetric cells at different current densities from 0.1 to 6 mA cm–2 with the corresponding capacities from 0.1 to 6 mAh cm–2. It is apparent that the assembled Zn@F-CDs cell shows a stable and low overpotential at each current density (33, 40, 46, 51, 63 and 80 mV at 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 4 and 6 mA cm–2), which are in sharp contrast with the Zn||Zn cell (Figs. S5-S6), showing that the F-CDs protective layer equips the symmetric cell with better rate capacity and reversibility [27].

It is noted that Zn corrosion is another inevitable parasitic reaction, which seriously affects the lifespan of batteries [28]. Hence, Tafel curves were recorded to further investigate the corrosion reaction on the surface of both electrodes. Fig. 3e displays that compared with the bare Zn anode, the value of corrosion current density of Zn@F-CDs anode reduces by 547 μA cm–2, indicating that the F-CDs layer can also effectively inhibit corrosion reaction. To further explore the anti-corrosion property of F-CDs protective layer, X-ray diffraction (XRD) tests of both two electrodes were conducted after soaking in 2 M ZnSO4 electrolyte for 3 days. As displayed in the XRD curves (Fig. S7), apparent peaks of corrosion products (Zn4SO4(OH)6·5 H2O, ZHS) can be observed in the bare Zn anode. Conversely, under the same condition, only small ZHS peaks can be seen in the Zn@F-CDs anode, further demonstrating that the Zn corrosion reaction can be effectively inhibited under the effect of the F-CDs layer.

XRD tests were conducted to explore the composition change of anode phase at 1 mA cm–2. A series of obvious peaks of by-products (ZHS) appear on the XRD pattern of the bare Zn anode after 15 cycles, while there are no visible ZHS peaks on the Zn@F-CDs anode at the same test condition (Fig. 3f), implying the better suppression of F-CDs protective layer on by-products. Meanwhile, it is well established that the crystallization orientation of Zn(002) is related to the compact deposition morphology, which is conducive to weakening HER and inhibiting formation of byproducts to achieve a stable Zn anode [29]. Fig. 3f also presents that the intensity ratio of I
(002)
/I
(101) in the cycled bare Zn anode is 0.484 and it is relatively low compared with the Zn@F-CDs anode (0.684) after 15 cycles, suggesting the F-CDs layer is able to facilitate the deposition of (002) plane. Moreover, it is clear to find that the preferential orientation of Zn(002) plane deposition on the Zn@F-CDs anode can be enhanced with the increased cycling numbers [30]. As depicted in Fig. S8, the intensity ratio of I
(002)
/I
(101
) of the F-CDs anode is 0.8251 after 37 cycles at 5 mA cm–2, 1 mAh cm–2, when the cycle numbers are 93 and 149, the intensity ratio of I
(002)
/I
(101) are about 1.228 and 1.551, respectively.

In addition to the symmetric cells, the performances of Zn||Cu half cells are also evaluated (Fig. 3g). The Zn@F-CDs anode delivers an excellent average coulombic efficiency (99.32%) over 1100 cycles. Contrarily, the CE of bare Zn anode is only 98.77% and fluctuates violently after 94 cycles, which can be ascribed to the rampant side reactions and short-circuit of the battery [31]. Besides, the low CE in the first few laps can be ascribed to the activation and interfacial wetting process [32,33]. Subsequently, the CV curves were also tested in Zn||Cu and Zn||Cu@F-CDs half cells. Compared with the bare Zn anode, the Zn||Cu@F-CDs half cell exhibits a higher current and a narrower overpotential at 1 mV s–1 (Fig. 3h), suggesting that the enhanced Zn2+ ions reaction kinetics and the reduced nucleation barrier.

Chromoamperometry measurements were also employed to study the mechanism of Zn diffusion and nucleation process in symmetric cells. As illustrated in Fig. 3i, under the potential of − 150 mV, the bare Zn anode shows a continuous increased current density associated with a 2D diffusion process where Zn ions apt to accumulate dendrites at the most energetically favorable sites to minimize the surface energy and rapid charge transfer [34]. By contrast, the Zn@F-CDs anode shows a short 2D diffusion process and a stable 3D diffusion process for the rest of time, suggesting that the multifunctional layer could guide the Zn2+ ions to migrate in lateral direction and nucleate in near the initial sites, thus leading to a uniform Zn deposition. This is attributed to the strong adsorption between zincophilic polar groups (-F, -CO and -CHO) in F-CDs coating layer and Zn ions [35].

In order to further investigate the influence of these three kinds of zincophilic groups of the F-CDs layer on Zn anode, we prepared two different kinds of CDs. The further detailed synthesis procedure can be found in the supporting information. It is worth to note that the CD-1 is a kind of common carbon dots based on aldol reaction between sodium hydroxide and acetaldhyde, while the CD-2 is synthesized based on intersection aldol condensation reaction of benzaldehyde, acetaldehyde and sodium hydroxide. As shown in Figs. S9-S10, only both -CHO and -CO groups can be observed in XPS results of CD-1 and CD-2 samples. That means CD-1 and CD-2 materials only have hydrophilic groups (-CHO and -CO). Moreover, the cycling performance of Zn@CD-1 and Zn@CD-2 anode in symmetric cells was also performed at the same condition. As shown in Fig. S11-S12, the cycle life of both Zn@CD-1||Zn@CD-1 cell and Zn@CD-2||Zn@CD-2 cell are longer than that of the bare Zn||Zn cell, while it is shorter than that of the Zn@F-CDs||Zn@F-CDs cell. There is no doubt that F-CDs protective layer endows Zn anode with the relatively best performance, indicating that -F group may play an essential role in stabilizing Zn anode. To further explore this, XPS etching tests were also conducted ( Fig. 4a). Before the Ar sputtering, a regular XPS test was performed on the cycled Zn@F-CDs anode surface (removed the F-CDs layer). It was found that ZnF2 was in-situ formed on the cycled electrode surface. The cycled Zn@F-CDs anode shows two typical Zn 2p peaks (Zn 2p1/2 peak at 1044.40 eV and Zn 2p3/2 peak at 1021.60 eV) and the Zn-F bonds,[32,36] which can be detected in Zn 2p spectrum. Moreover, the F 1 s spectrum of the Zn@F-CDs surface shows the two evident peaks, especially the peak at 684.5 eV is consistent with the feature of ZnF2, confirming the formation of ZnF2 on the Zn anode surface.[37] Subsequently, upon 50 s of sputtering, it is apparent to see that the peak corresponding to the ZnF2 becomes more obvious and the peak ascribed to the C-F bond disappears. The XPS etching test exhibits that the ZnF2 peaks can be observed clearly in the cycled Zn@F-CDs surface (removed the layer), indicating ZnF2 can in-situ form between Zn and the F-CDs layer during the cycling process.

Moreover, high resolution-transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) was utilized to further prove the existence of the ZnF2 phase, and the surface of cycled Zn@F-CDs anode was removed to prepare the TEM sample. The HR-TEM image shows two representative lattice fringes with the spacing of 0.247 and 0.23 nm respectively, which match well with the (002) and (100) planes of Zn (PDF#04–0831). Most importantly, the lattice spacing of 0.31 and 0.248 nm is indexed to the (111) and (020) planes of ZnF2 (PDF#21–1480) (Fig. 4b). Meanwhile, the selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern displays diffraction rings, which are assigned to the (111), (310) and (200) plane of ZnF2 and the (110), (002) and (104) plane of Zn (Fig. 4c). In addition, the high-angel annular dark field (HAADF) scanning TEM image and corresponding element mapping can further confirm the existence of ZnF2. As shown in Fig. 4d, it is obvious to observe that the Zn and F distribute evenly on the whole Zn flake, while no C and O upon on it, suggesting the -F group of F-CDs protective layer has an interaction with Zn2+ to the formation ZnF2. It is established that ZnF2 can play an important role in homogenizing Zn2+ ions deposition, which can further explain why the Zn@F-CDs anode possesses better cycling performance and stability.

Real-time observation images of both electrodes were collected on in-situ optical microscopy at 6 mA cm–2. As exhibited in Fig. 5a, the surface of the bare Zn anode is smooth at the beginning. Not surprisingly, some messy protrusions emerge on the surface after 5 min and gradually grow up in the following time because of the known “tip effect”. Uneven Zn deposition and amounts of Zn dendrites are detected on the bare Zn anode at 20 min, which are fatal to electrochemical performance and cycling span life of batteries.[38] On the contrary, the relatively even Zn deposition can be seen on the Zn@F-CDs surface throughout the whole plating process (Fig. 5b), indicating the F-CDs layer can achieve uniform Zn deposition and inhibit Zn dendrites [39]. Subsequently, electron probe microanalysis (EPMA) recorded the concentration of Zn, O and S elements on different electrode surfaces. After 15 cycles at 1 mA cm–2, 1 mAh cm–2, the cycled bare Zn anode and Zn@F-CDs (removing the layer) anode exhibit higher concentration of O element and lower concentration of Zn element compared with the uncycled Zn anode (Figs. S13-S15), which is attributed to the arbitrary formation of by-products (ZHS) during the cycling process [25]. Noting that the cycled bare Zn anode shows less Zn concentration (69.1% vs 85.0%) and more S concentration (4.8% vs 0.69%) compared with the Zn@F-CDs anode, implying the F-CDs coating indeed suppresses the formation of ZHS (Fig. 5c-f).

Moreover, SEM was also conducted to record the surface and cross-sectional images of two anodes after different cycles, proving the suppression effect of the F-CDs layer on Zn dendrites growth. As displayed in Fig. 6a-d, the Zn surface becomes rough with amounts of random Zn flakes and small dendrites after 15 cycles at 1 mA cm–2, which turns more severe and grows into lots of large dendrites after 50 cycles at the same test condition. The same phenomenon can be observed by SEM images with high magnification. In sharp contrast, a flat and smooth surface can be detected on the Zn@F-CDs anode after removing the F-CDs layer after 15 cycles and it remains dendrite-free surface morphology after 50 cycles (Fig. 6e-h), which may benefit from that functional groups on the surface of F-CDs provide more nucleation sites to regulate Zn2+ ions deposition. Moreover, as displayed in Fig. S16, cross-sectional SEM images of two anodes show the same results as surface SEM images. It is clear to see that uneven Zn deposition and disordered Zn dendrites are covered on the bare Zn anode, while dense deposition and smooth surface on the cycled Zn@F-CDs anode were observed. Meanwhile, optical microscopy was also employed to obtain 3D height images. The bare Zn anode shows irregular protuberances and a rough surface with a maximum height of 63.8 µm (Fig. 6 i1–i3). On the contrary, the cycled Zn@F-CDs anode after removing the F-CDs layer displays a relatively flat surface morphology with a maximum height of 14.5 µm (Fig. 6j1–j3), again demonstrating that the even Zn deposition and inhibited zinc dendrites under the effect of the F-CDs protective layer.

To investigate the application potential of F-CDs layer, Zn@F-CDs anode and bare Zn anode paired with NH4V4O10 cathode were used to assemble full cells. Especially, the NH4V4O10 cathode materials were prepared according to the previous report [40]. Moreover, it can be seen that its morphology and structure are displayed in Fig. S17. The CV curves show that the Zn@F-CDs||NH4V4O10 full cell shows similar oxidation/reduction peaks with the Zn||NH4V4O10 full cell, while the current density in the Zn@F-CDs||NH4V4O10 full cell is higher compared with the Zn||NH4V4O10 full cell ( Fig. 7a), manifesting the introduction of F-CDs protective layer could enhance redox kinetics [33]. What’s more, the SEM images of two anodes after CV test harvesting from the full cells are collected (Fig. S18). As expected, the Zn anode without a protective layer shows a rough surface with some holes. In addition, it can be seen clearly from the SEM images at larger magnification that a lot of small dendrites accumulate on the surface. XRD curves of full cells show similar variation trends with symmetric cells (Fig. 7b). After 60 cycles at 2 A g–1, note that the high intensity of Zn4SO4(OH)6·5 H2O diffraction peaks can be detected in the bare Zn anode. Contrarily, there are only low signals of Zn4SO4(OH)6·5 H2O in the Zn@F-CDs anode, which suggests the F-CDs layer indeed has a good protective effect on the full cell.

The self-discharge behavior of aqueous full cells is a common phenomenon, it greatly affects the CE and causes irreversible electrolyte consumption. To evaluate the influence of F-CDs layer on self-discharge behavior, the assembled full cells are charged to 1.6 V and go through 48 h rest. As shown in Figs. 7c and S19, the Zn@F-CDs||NH4V4O10 full cell keeps 91.41% of its original capacity, which is higher contrast to the Zn||NH4V4O10 full cell (87.17%). Furthermore, Figs. 7d and S20 depict that the Zn@F-CDs||NH4V4O10 full cell possesses a higher specific capacity of the rate capability at corresponding current densities compared with the Zn||NH4V4O10 full cell, which may attribute to the smaller charge-transfer resistance (Fig. S21). Subsequently, the SEM images are recorded after the rate test (Fig. 7e1 and 7e2). A mount of the anisotropic hexagonal nanosheets could be observed in the bare Zn anode, while a flat and relatively dense surface can be obtained after removing the protective layer in the Zn@F-CDs anode surface, implying the effective suppression of by-products. Furthermore, the long-term cycling stability of the Zn@F-CDs||NH4V4O10 and Zn||NH4V4O10 full cells at 1 A g–1 are shown in Fig. 7f. The bare Zn||NH4V4O10 full cell only displays a specific capacity of 123.0 mAh g–1 after 400 cycles and a higher specific capacity of 166.0 mAh g–1 can be acquired in the Zn@F-CDs||NH4V4O10 full cell after 500 cycles. The same phenomenon can also be obtained at 2 A g–1 (Fig. S22). Especially, there are violent fluctuations in the long time cycling curve of Zn||NH4V4O10 full cell after 1000 cycles, while the Zn@F-CDs||NH4V4O10 full cell can operate stably up to 2000 cycles with a higher specific capacity.

In summary, under the guidance of theoretical calculations, a hydrophobic and zincophilic multifunctional F-CDs protective layer is designed to inhibit HER, Zn corrosion and the growth of Zn dendrites for further application of ZIBs. The obtained artificial F-CDs layer with three kinds of functional groups (-CHO, -CO and -F) has strong adsorption with Zn2+ ions, leading to uniform Zn deposition. Especially, the -F group benefits to the in-situ formation of ZnF2 interlayer between the pristine F-CDs protective layer and Zn anode surface, which is the excellent Zn2+ conductor. In addition, the bilayer enables a restrained 2D diffusion process and leads to the preferred Zn(002) plane deposition. Consequently, the symmetric cell with Zn@F-CDs electrode achieves better stability over 3500 h at 1 mA cm–2. Interestingly, under the effect of the F-CDs protective layer, half cells realize a stable cycling performance over 1150 cycles with an average CE of 99.32%. What’s more, the modified Zn anode delivers enhanced electrochemical performance and rate performance paired with NH4V4O10 cathode. There is no doubt that the construction of F-CDs layer provides perceptive strategies for the interface engineering of the Zn anode.

Zhaofei Ge: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing − original draft. Laiqiang Xu: Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology. Yunlong Xu: Formal analysis. Jiae Wu: Methodology, Formal analysis. Zhenglei Geng: Methodology. Xiangting Xiao: Methodology. Wentao Deng: Formal analysis. Guoqiang Zou: Software. Hongshuai Hou: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Supervision, Writing − review & editing. Xiaobo Ji: Funding acquisition, Resources, Writing − review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Science and Technology Innovation Program of Hunan Province (2021RC3014), the Graduate Independent Innovation Exploration Program of Central South University (2023ZZTS0735), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52074359, U21A20284, 52261135632).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.nanoen.2023.109053.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Zhaofei Ge is now a master student under at College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University. She received her Bachelor of Engineering degree from Henan Normal University in 2021. Her current research focuses on the interfacial design of zinc anodes for aqueous zinc-ion batteries.

Laiqiang Xu is currently a Ph.D. candidate at the College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University. He received his Master degree from Shanghai University in 2017. His research interests focus on the synthesis and application of solid electrolytes for rechargeable batteries.

Yunlong Xu is now a master student under at College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University. He received his Bachelor of Engineering degree from Jiangxi University of Science and Technology in 2021. His current research focuses on advanced materials and lithium ion battery recycling.

Jiae Wu is a master’s candidate at College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University. She received her Bachelor of Engineering degree from Guizhou University in 2021. Her current research focuses on the interfacial design of zinc anodes for aqueous zinc-ion batteries.

Zhenglei Geng is now a master student under at College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University. He received his Bachelor of Engineering degree from Central South University in 2023. His current research focuses on CDs modified layered materials.

Xiangting Xiao is a master candidate at the College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University. She received her B.E. degree from Xiangtan University in 2021. Her research interests focus on deciphering the electric eel biomimetics for novel power sources.

Dr. Wentao Deng is a Lecturer at the College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University. She received his Ph.D. at University of Bath in 2017. Her current research interests are advanced energy materials and storage devices including lithium/sodium ion batteries and Supercapacitors based on structured-materials.

Dr. Guoqiang Zou is an associate professor at the College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University. He received his Ph.D. from Central South University in 2018. His current research interests are electrochemistry and key materials for electrochemical energy storage devices.

Dr. Hongshuai Hou is a Professor at the College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University. He received his Ph.D. at Central South University in 2016. His current research interests are electrochemistry and key materials for electrochemical energy storage devices.

Dr. Xiaobo Ji is a “Shenghua” Professor at Central South University and a Fellow of the Royal Society of Chemistry, specializing in the research and development of batteries and supercapacitor materials and their systems. He received his PhD in Electrochemistry in 2007 under the supervision of Prof. Richard Compton at the University of Oxford and undertook postdoctoral work at MIT with Prof. Donald Sadoway.
